title: Buffers
subtitle: Files in memory, navigated with `:ls` and `:b`.
summary: Vim loads each file into a buffer. `:ls` lists all buffers. `:b` switches to one by name or number. `:bn` / `:bp` step through them. `:bd` unloads a buffer.

blocks.text: Every file you open in Vim becomes a *buffer* — an in-memory copy. You may have ten buffers loaded but only one window showing one of them. Navigating buffers, not windows, is how you move between files.

blocks.title: What `:ls` Shows You
blocks.text:
`:ls` (alias `:buffers`) lists every buffer Vim knows about, with flags:

  • `%` — current buffer.
  • `#` — alternate (previous) buffer.
  • `+` — modified.
  • `a` — active (loaded and shown in some window).
  • `h` — hidden (loaded but not shown).
  • `-` — modifiable off.
  • `=` — read-only.

You can switch by buffer number (`:b 5`) or by name fragment (`:b foo`). Tab-complete works.

blocks.title: The 'hidden' Option Is Worth Setting
blocks.text:
By default Vim refuses to switch buffers if the current one is modified. Set `:set hidden` (it's on by default in Neovim) and Vim will *hide* the modified buffer instead — keeping the changes in memory while you work on another file.

Without `'hidden'`, you'll constantly hit "E37: No write since last change" and end up using `:b!` (force) or aborting. With `'hidden'`, buffers behave like the tabs in any modern editor — switch freely, save when ready.

The trade-off is that you can `:q` a window with unsaved buffers in the background. `:qall` will warn you about them when exiting Vim.
{key:Ctrl-^} swaps to the *alternate* buffer (the one you were just in). It's the buffer-level version of {key:`}{key:`}. Most people use this dozens of times a day.

blocks.title: Buffer state and window state are independent layers
blocks.text: A buffer owns the text and the modified flag. A window owns a viewport (top line, leftmost column) and a cursor position. The same buffer can be open in multiple windows simultaneously, each with its own cursor. Closing a window doesn't close its buffer — buffers persist hidden until explicitly deleted with `:bd`. Internally these are two object pools with a many-to-many relationship.
